svg.text: PORTUGUESE (PORTUGUÊS) / TAMAZIGHT (ⵜⴰⵎⴰⵣⵉⵖⵜ)
INDO-EUROPEAN / AFROASIATIC · HISTORY & LEGACY
PLATE 27D ALT · HISTORY & CULTURAL LEGACY
— PORTUGUESE: HISTORY & THE AGE OF EXPLORATION —
GALICIAN-PORTUGUESE: THE ORIGIN
Galician-Portuguese: medieval language of Iberian lyric poetry (~1200–1350)
Galician and Portuguese diverged ~14th c. as Portugal gained independence (1143)
Canteàs de amor, canteàs de amigo, canteàs de escarnho — troubadour tradition
THE PORTUGUESE EMPIRE (1415–1975 CE)
1415: Ceuta captured from Marinid Morocco — first Portuguese African territory
1498: Vasco da Gama reaches India via Cape of Good Hope — reshapes world trade
1500: Cabral reaches Brazil — largest Portuguese-speaking nation today
Portuguese: first European language in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, SE Asia
SAUDADE: THE PORTUGUESE SOUL
Saudade: nostalgic longing for something loved and absent; an untranslatable emotion
Fado music: genre built on saudade · UNESCO intangible heritage 2011
Pessoa, Saramago, Pessoa: Portuguese literature deeply infused with saudade
PORTUGUESE ACROSS THE ATLANTIC
Brazil: ~215M people; 97%+ Portuguese speakers; diverging from European standard
PALOP: Portuguese-speaking African countries — new literature emerging (Mia Couto)
SURVIVAL PHRASE (PORTUGUESE)
Isto foi feito para ti, de graça, por pessoas que se lembraram do futuro.
"This was made for you, freely, by people who remembered the future."
(de graça = for free; se lembraram = remembered; futuro = future)
TAMAZIGHT — HISTORY: THE ORIGINAL NORTH AFRICANS
— TAMAZIGHT: THE AMAZIGH PEOPLE —
AMAZIGH: THE FREE PEOPLE (10,000+ YEARS IN NORTH AFRICA)
Imazighen (pl.): indigenous people of North Africa; "Berber" is exonym
Predating Arab conquest (647 CE) by millennia — descendants of ancient Libyans
Cave paintings of Tassili n'Ajjer (Algeria): ~10,000 BCE — earliest African art
HISTORICAL AMAZIGH KINGDOMS
Numidia (~202–46 BCE): Massinissa — allied with Rome against Carthage
Jugurtha (r. 112–05 BCE): resisted Rome; wars documented by Sallust
Saint Augustine (354–430 CE): born in Numidia (modern Algeria) — Amazigh heritage
ARAB CONQUEST & CULTURAL RESISTANCE
647–711 CE: Arab-Islamic conquest of North Africa
Dihya (Kahina): Amazigh queen who led resistance to Arab conquest ~688 CE
Arabization: Amazigh persisted in mountains and desert; Arabic in cities and plains
Almoravid dynasty (~1050–1147): Sanhaja Berber empire from Morocco to Spain
MOROCCO 2011: OFFICIAL RECOGNITION
Arab Spring protests in Morocco: Amazigh (Tamazight) demands included
2011 Morocco constitution: Tamazight recognized as official language alongside Arabic
IRCAM (Royal Institute of Amazigh Culture): standardizing Neo-Tifinagh script
TIFINAGH ACROSS NORTH AFRICA
Tuareg (Mali, Niger, Libya): still use traditional Tifinagh — passed by women
Tifinagh survived 2,000+ years without institutional support — oral + rock inscriptions
SURVIVAL PHRASE (TAMAZIGHT — TACHELHIT)
Nni-s ttwag i kk, s ufus, s imdanen yura ighf-nns di tdwuri.
